title: Critical Limb Ischaemia
oneLiner: Severe peripheral arterial disease causing rest pain, tissue loss (ulcer/gangrene) for >2 weeks; high risk of amputation and cardiovascular death.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute limb ischaemia** – the time-critical mimic. Key finding: acute onset (<14 days) or sudden worsening of pain with ONE of motor deficit (foot drop, no toe movement) or sensory loss (absent pinprick). This is a surgical emergency: needs immediate vascular review, formal angiogram ± embolectomy/thrombectomy. Do NOT wait for ABI in this scenario.
- **Sepsis from infected ischaemic tissue** – key feature: spreading cellulitis, systemic inflammatory response (tachycardia, fever, hypotension), crepitus or foul discharge suggests gas gangrene. This needs urgent surgical debridement, IV antibiotics, and high-dependency care.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Rest pain – typically in forefoot, toes, dorsum of foot; worse at night, relieved by hanging leg over side of bed
  - Non-healing ulcer (>2 weeks) or focal gangrene (toe, heel, nail bed)
  - Patient may report the leg feels cold, or that they cannot feel pulses
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Absent palpable pulses (femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial)
  - Capillary refill >5 seconds
  - Elevation pallor (leg pale on raising) and dependency rubor (red on lowering)
  - Buerger’s test positive (foot becomes pale on elevation, then hyperaemic when dependent)
  - Ulcer/gangrene – note clean vs sloughy/necrotic, presence of drainage, odour
  - Check sensation and motor function in all toes – any deficit = acute deterioration
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - Chronic venous insufficiency (varicose veins, haemosiderin deposition, ulcers with sloping edges, normal pulses)
  - Diabetic neuropathy (painless ulcer, absent sensation, pulses usually present – unless combined arterial disease)
  - Charcot foot (painless, swollen, warm, bony deformity, normal pulses)
  - Cellulitis without ischaemia (systemic signs, spreading erythema, normal pulses and ABI)
  - Livedoid vasculopathy / vasculitis (non-healing ulcer with livedo reticularis, systemic symptoms)
- **Splitter:** “Does the ulcer/rest pain improve with the leg hanging down?” In CLI – yes, because gravity improves perfusion. In venous disease – no difference or worse (gravity worsens oedema). Also, check ABI: <0.5 strongly points to CLI.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Ankle-brachial pressure index (ABI) – use Doppler probe, inflate cuff above malleoli, measure systolic pressures. In CLI, ABI <0.5 (or <0.4 for tissue loss). If >1.3 (non-compressible vessels – consider toe pressures).
  - Toe pressures (hallux, if Doppler available) – <30-50 mmHg suggestive of CLI
  - Waveform analysis – triphasic normal, biphasic-mild, monophasic-severe
  - Wound swab if clinically infected (not routine)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&Es, CRP, clotting screen, glucose, HbA1c (diabetes control), lipid profile, peripheral blood film (if vasculitis suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: Duplex ultrasound (arterial mapping from aorta to foot)
  - Second-line: CT angiogram (if revascularisation planned and renal function allows, otherwise [check local policy: consider MRA or CO2 angio])
  - Plain X-ray of foot – to rule out osteomyelitis, Charcot joint, gas in tissues
- **Never-miss test:** Formal opinion and vascular imaging (duplex or CTA) – omitting this delays revascularisation and risks irreversible limb loss. In a patient with new motor/sensory loss, an immediate handheld Doppler check (absence of audible signal at foot) is a red flag for acute ischaemia.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Analgesia (paracetamol + opioids if severe)
  - Keep leg in dependent position (do not elevate) – place pillow under mattress to allow leg to hang slightly down; avoids worsening ischaemia
  - Contact vascular surgical registrar on call – referral MUST be same day (urgent outpatient or admission)
  - If signs of acute limb ischaemia (motor/sensory loss) – call vascular reg immediately, discuss heparin infusion ([check local policy]), keep NBM for potential urgent surgery/angiogram
  - If ulcer/gangrene with sepsis – start IV antibiotics (e.g. co-amoxiclav 1.2g TDS, metronidazole 500mg TDS, or piperacillin-tazobactam if severe – adjust per local guidelines), fluid resuscitation, blood cultures
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Complete cardiovascular risk assessment – ECG, echo if murmur/aortic pathology
  - Optimise medical therapy: antiplatelet, high-intensity statin, BP control (target <140/90), glucose control (target HbA1c <53 mmol/mol if diabetic)
  - Wound care – daily review, gentle saline or hydrogel dressings; avoid compression bandages, avoid soaking foot
  - If revascularisation planned – requires discussion with radiology/vascular surgery; consider endovascular (angioplasty ± stent) vs surgical bypass
  - Consider thrombolysis only if acute-on-chronic with no distal runoff – must be discussed with tertiary vascular centre
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Smoking cessation – refer to stop smoking service, offer NRT and varenicline/bupropion per local formulary
  - Antiplatelet (clopidogrel 75mg OD lifelong unless contraindicated)
  - Statin (atorvastatin 80mg OD)
  - Diabetes control – review by diabetic foot team
  - Supervised exercise programme (if no rest pain) – but CLI patients often cannot exercise
  - Regular footcare – chiropody, offloading footwear
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: NBM if revascularisation planned; otherwise oral intake as tolerated (IV fluids only if dehydrated/septic)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD if low bleeding risk (avoid if acute limb ischaemia due to risk of haematoma)
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2, plus hourly visual check of limb colour and temperature in first 24h
  - Diet: NBM after midnight if any intervention possible; otherwise normal diet with diabetes check
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Clopidogrel 75mg PO OD (long-term) – preferred over aspirin in CLI (CAPRIE evidence, also NICE CG147)
  - Atorvastatin 80mg PO OD (high-intensity statin) – regardless of baseline LDL
  - Analgesia: Paracetamol 1g PO QDS; if inadequate add codeine 30-60mg PO QDS (max 240mg/day) or morphine sulphate immediate-release 5-10mg PO 4-hourly PRN (caution in renal impairment)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Aspirin 75mg PO OD if clopidogrel intolerant (e.g. allergy)
  - Simvastatin or rosuvastatin if atorvastatin not tolerated (doses per local formulary)
  - For acute limb ischaemia: Heparin infusion – [check local policy] (typically unfractionated heparin 5000 units IV bolus then infusion at 1000-1500 units/hour adjusted to aPTT 1.5-2.5x control)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Clopidogrel – caution with NSAIDs (GI bleed), PPIs (especially omeprazole – use pantoprazole if needed)
  - Atorvastatin – avoid strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin, itraconazole), increased myopathy risk
  - Heparin – avoid in active bleeding, recent major surgery, thrombocytopenia (HIT)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Clopidogrel – no dose adjustment, but caution in severe renal impairment (increased bleeding risk)
  - Atorvastatin – no adjustment for mild-moderate renal impairment, avoid if active liver disease or elevated transaminases >3x ULN
  - Heparin – dose reduce if eGFR <30 (use LMWH with caution; for continuous IV infusion, [check local protocol for nomogram])
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Heparin infusion** – starting without a baseline aPTT and repeat within 6 hours. Omission of monitoring leads to under- or over-anticoagulation → thrombosis or major bleed.
  - **Clopidogrel** – prescribing without checking for dual antiplatelet therapy or bleeding risk in the context of revascularisation (risk of surgical site haematoma).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Irreversible limb ischaemia → amputation (major vs minor)
  - Rhabdomyolysis (from severe ischaemia) → AKI
  - Sepsis from infected gangrene / necrotising fasciitis
  - Acute MI/stroke (CLI is a coronary heart disease equivalent – perioperative risk)
- **Chronic:**
  - Wound infection, chronic osteomyelitis
  - Chronic pain, phantom limb pain after amputation
  - Depression, loss of mobility and independence
sections.Prognosis: - Within one year, ~25% of CLI patients require major amputation and ~20% die (primarily from cardiovascular events). Revascularisation improves limb salvage rates to 70-80% but long-term survival remains poor.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do assess toe pressures** – if ABI is non-compressible (>1.3) due to calcified vessels (diabetes/renal failure), toe pressures give the true perfusion. <30 mmHg = critical.
- **Do keep the leg dependent** – simple non-pharmacological intervention relieves rest pain and buys time. Elevation worsens pain and may extend tissue loss.
- **Do involve the diabetic foot team early** – they can arrange offloading, aggressive wound care, and coordinate revascularisation if needed.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not elevate the leg** – a common confuser: raising the legs is good for venous oedema but dangerous in CLI (reduced perfusion pressure precipitates further ischaemia).
- **Do not use compression bandaging** – even if the ulcer looks wet, compression reduces arterial inflow and may cause acute decompensation.
- **Do not assume pain is simply neuropathy** – in a diabetic patient with an ulcer, always check pulses and ABI; rest pain can mimic neuropathy and delay referral.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Pain worsens, becomes constant, or new rest pain develops
  - Any colour change (blue/pale mottling) spreads beyond current area
  - Fever, increasing redness, or foul discharge from the ulcer
  - Inability to bear weight or move toes
- **Follow-up:**
  - Vascular clinic urgent appointment (within 1-2 weeks) or sooner if pain uncontrolled
  - Wound care review by district nurse / podiatrist within 48 hours
  - Stop smoking service and GP for risk factor management within 1 month
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call vascular surgical registrar immediately if:**
  - Any motor or sensory deficit (foot drop, inability to wiggle toes) – this indicates acute limb ischaemia
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or significant haemodynamic instability
  - Lactate >2.0 mmol/L (if measured) – suggests tissue ischaemia
  - Sudden loss of previously audible Doppler signal
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**
  - Signs of sepsis (NEWS2 >7, lactate >4, shock requiring vasopressors) – need pre-op optimisation and vascular anaesthesia input

rawMarkdown:
## Critical Limb Ischaemia
**One-liner:** Severe peripheral arterial disease causing rest pain, tissue loss (ulcer/gangrene) for >2 weeks; high risk of amputation and cardiovascular death.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute limb ischaemia** – the time-critical mimic. Key finding: acute onset (<14 days) or sudden worsening of pain with ONE of motor deficit (foot drop, no toe movement) or sensory loss (absent pinprick). This is a surgical emergency: needs immediate vascular review, formal angiogram ± embolectomy/thrombectomy. Do NOT wait for ABI in this scenario.
- **Sepsis from infected ischaemic tissue** – key feature: spreading cellulitis, systemic inflammatory response (tachycardia, fever, hypotension), crepitus or foul discharge suggests gas gangrene. This needs urgent surgical debridement, IV antibiotics, and high-dependency care.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Rest pain – typically in forefoot, toes, dorsum of foot; worse at night, relieved by hanging leg over side of bed
  - Non-healing ulcer (>2 weeks) or focal gangrene (toe, heel, nail bed)
  - Patient may report the leg feels cold, or that they cannot feel pulses
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Absent palpable pulses (femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial)
  - Capillary refill >5 seconds
  - Elevation pallor (leg pale on raising) and dependency rubor (red on lowering)
  - Buerger’s test positive (foot becomes pale on elevation, then hyperaemic when dependent)
  - Ulcer/gangrene – note clean vs sloughy/necrotic, presence of drainage, odour
  - Check sensation and motor function in all toes – any deficit = acute deterioration

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - Chronic venous insufficiency (varicose veins, haemosiderin deposition, ulcers with sloping edges, normal pulses)
  - Diabetic neuropathy (painless ulcer, absent sensation, pulses usually present – unless combined arterial disease)
  - Charcot foot (painless, swollen, warm, bony deformity, normal pulses)
  - Cellulitis without ischaemia (systemic signs, spreading erythema, normal pulses and ABI)
  - Livedoid vasculopathy / vasculitis (non-healing ulcer with livedo reticularis, systemic symptoms)
- **Splitter:** “Does the ulcer/rest pain improve with the leg hanging down?” In CLI – yes, because gravity improves perfusion. In venous disease – no difference or worse (gravity worsens oedema). Also, check ABI: <0.5 strongly points to CLI.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Ankle-brachial pressure index (ABI) – use Doppler probe, inflate cuff above malleoli, measure systolic pressures. In CLI, ABI <0.5 (or <0.4 for tissue loss). If >1.3 (non-compressible vessels – consider toe pressures).
  - Toe pressures (hallux, if Doppler available) – <30-50 mmHg suggestive of CLI
  - Waveform analysis – triphasic normal, biphasic-mild, monophasic-severe
  - Wound swab if clinically infected (not routine)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&Es, CRP, clotting screen, glucose, HbA1c (diabetes control), lipid profile, peripheral blood film (if vasculitis suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: Duplex ultrasound (arterial mapping from aorta to foot)
  - Second-line: CT angiogram (if revascularisation planned and renal function allows, otherwise [check local policy: consider MRA or CO2 angio])
  - Plain X-ray of foot – to rule out osteomyelitis, Charcot joint, gas in tissues
- **Never-miss test:** Formal opinion and vascular imaging (duplex or CTA) – omitting this delays revascularisation and risks irreversible limb loss. In a patient with new motor/sensory loss, an immediate handheld Doppler check (absence of audible signal at foot) is a red flag for acute ischaemia.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Analgesia (paracetamol + opioids if severe)
  - Keep leg in dependent position (do not elevate) – place pillow under mattress to allow leg to hang slightly down; avoids worsening ischaemia
  - Contact vascular surgical registrar on call – referral MUST be same day (urgent outpatient or admission)
  - If signs of acute limb ischaemia (motor/sensory loss) – call vascular reg immediately, discuss heparin infusion ([check local policy]), keep NBM for potential urgent surgery/angiogram
  - If ulcer/gangrene with sepsis – start IV antibiotics (e.g. co-amoxiclav 1.2g TDS, metronidazole 500mg TDS, or piperacillin-tazobactam if severe – adjust per local guidelines), fluid resuscitation, blood cultures
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Complete cardiovascular risk assessment – ECG, echo if murmur/aortic pathology
  - Optimise medical therapy: antiplatelet, high-intensity statin, BP control (target <140/90), glucose control (target HbA1c <53 mmol/mol if diabetic)
  - Wound care – daily review, gentle saline or hydrogel dressings; avoid compression bandages, avoid soaking foot
  - If revascularisation planned – requires discussion with radiology/vascular surgery; consider endovascular (angioplasty ± stent) vs surgical bypass
  - Consider thrombolysis only if acute-on-chronic with no distal runoff – must be discussed with tertiary vascular centre
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Smoking cessation – refer to stop smoking service, offer NRT and varenicline/bupropion per local formulary
  - Antiplatelet (clopidogrel 75mg OD lifelong unless contraindicated)
  - Statin (atorvastatin 80mg OD)
  - Diabetes control – review by diabetic foot team
  - Supervised exercise programme (if no rest pain) – but CLI patients often cannot exercise
  - Regular footcare – chiropody, offloading footwear
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: NBM if revascularisation planned; otherwise oral intake as tolerated (IV fluids only if dehydrated/septic)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD if low bleeding risk (avoid if acute limb ischaemia due to risk of haematoma)
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2, plus hourly visual check of limb colour and temperature in first 24h
  - Diet: NBM after midnight if any intervention possible; otherwise normal diet with diabetes check

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Clopidogrel 75mg PO OD (long-term) – preferred over aspirin in CLI (CAPRIE evidence, also NICE CG147)
  - Atorvastatin 80mg PO OD (high-intensity statin) – regardless of baseline LDL
  - Analgesia: Paracetamol 1g PO QDS; if inadequate add codeine 30-60mg PO QDS (max 240mg/day) or morphine sulphate immediate-release 5-10mg PO 4-hourly PRN (caution in renal impairment)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Aspirin 75mg PO OD if clopidogrel intolerant (e.g. allergy)
  - Simvastatin or rosuvastatin if atorvastatin not tolerated (doses per local formulary)
  - For acute limb ischaemia: Heparin infusion – [check local policy] (typically unfractionated heparin 5000 units IV bolus then infusion at 1000-1500 units/hour adjusted to aPTT 1.5-2.5x control)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Clopidogrel – caution with NSAIDs (GI bleed), PPIs (especially omeprazole – use pantoprazole if needed)
  - Atorvastatin – avoid strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin, itraconazole), increased myopathy risk
  - Heparin – avoid in active bleeding, recent major surgery, thrombocytopenia (HIT)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Clopidogrel – no dose adjustment, but caution in severe renal impairment (increased bleeding risk)
  - Atorvastatin – no adjustment for mild-moderate renal impairment, avoid if active liver disease or elevated transaminases >3x ULN
  - Heparin – dose reduce if eGFR <30 (use LMWH with caution; for continuous IV infusion, [check local protocol for nomogram])
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Heparin infusion** – starting without a baseline aPTT and repeat within 6 hours. Omission of monitoring leads to under- or over-anticoagulation → thrombosis or major bleed.
  - **Clopidogrel** – prescribing without checking for dual antiplatelet therapy or bleeding risk in the context of revascularisation (risk of surgical site haematoma).

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Irreversible limb ischaemia → amputation (major vs minor)
  - Rhabdomyolysis (from severe ischaemia) → AKI
  - Sepsis from infected gangrene / necrotising fasciitis
  - Acute MI/stroke (CLI is a coronary heart disease equivalent – perioperative risk)
- **Chronic:**
  - Wound infection, chronic osteomyelitis
  - Chronic pain, phantom limb pain after amputation
  - Depression, loss of mobility and independence

### Prognosis
- Within one year, ~25% of CLI patients require major amputation and ~20% die (primarily from cardiovascular events). Revascularisation improves limb salvage rates to 70-80% but long-term survival remains poor.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do assess toe pressures** – if ABI is non-compressible (>1.3) due to calcified vessels (diabetes/renal failure), toe pressures give the true perfusion. <30 mmHg = critical.
- **Do keep the leg dependent** – simple non-pharmacological intervention relieves rest pain and buys time. Elevation worsens pain and may extend tissue loss.
- **Do involve the diabetic foot team early** – they can arrange offloading, aggressive wound care, and coordinate revascularisation if needed.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not elevate the leg** – a common confuser: raising the legs is good for venous oedema but dangerous in CLI (reduced perfusion pressure precipitates further ischaemia).
- **Do not use compression bandaging** – even if the ulcer looks wet, compression reduces arterial inflow and may cause acute decompensation.
- **Do not assume pain is simply neuropathy** – in a diabetic patient with an ulcer, always check pulses and ABI; rest pain can mimic neuropathy and delay referral.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Pain worsens, becomes constant, or new rest pain develops
  - Any colour change (blue/pale mottling) spreads beyond current area
  - Fever, increasing redness, or foul discharge from the ulcer
  - Inability to bear weight or move toes
- **Follow-up:**
  - Vascular clinic urgent appointment (within 1-2 weeks) or sooner if pain uncontrolled
  - Wound care review by district nurse / podiatrist within 48 hours
  - Stop smoking service and GP for risk factor management within 1 month

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call vascular surgical registrar immediately if:**
  - Any motor or sensory deficit (foot drop, inability to wiggle toes) – this indicates acute limb ischaemia
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or significant haemodynamic instability
  - Lactate >2.0 mmol/L (if measured) – suggests tissue ischaemia
  - Sudden loss of previously audible Doppler signal
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**
  - Signs of sepsis (NEWS2 >7, lactate >4, shock requiring vasopressors) – need pre-op optimisation and vascular anaesthesia input
